content.full_text:
CalifOrnia
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 24, 199 0
RaLph J. Grant
Treasurer
Elihu Harris for Mayor comnittee
505 - 14th Street, Suite 95O
Oakland, CA 946L2
Re: Your Request for fnformal Assi-stance
Our File No. I-9 O-1,4 4
Dear Mr. Grant:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice with
respect to the carnpaign. reporting provisions of the Political,
Reform Act (the Actr).r/ 
Please note, however, that formal writ-
ten advice is necessarily fact dependent. Consequently, Regula-
tion 18329(b) (8) (D) (copy enclosed) provides that formal written
advice will be declined s/here the questions are hypothetical in
nature. Thus, r,re can only^grovide these general guidelines with
respect to your questions . z /
OUESTIONS
1. If a partnership does not nake any contributions to
candidates, does the Act restrict the abitity of the individual
partners to contribute?
2. If a partnership contributes S1,0oo to a candidate, does
the Act restrict the ability of the individual partners to
contribute to the same candidate?
3. Do the ans$rers change if trro of the partners are husband
and wife?
L/ covernment code sections glooo-9101-5. Arr statutory refer-
ences are to the Governnent Code unless otherwise indicated. com-
rnission regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
innunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83LL4; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacrarnento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322.5660


File NO. I-90-144
Page 2
4. Do the answers change if there are onl,y two partners
rather than three or rnore?
CONCLUSTONS
1. Individuals, whether members of a partnership or not, nay
contribute up to S1,0o0 to a candidate in a fiscal year. However,
the Act requires the cumulation of contributions where one person
directs and controls the contributions of another person.
2. where a partnership is rnade up of tl.ro or more controlling
owners with equal ownership and control- over the partnership,
cutnulation of contrj,butions with the individual partners is not
required. Thus, the partnership and the controlling partners may
independentty nake the maxirnum contributions allowed by law to the
same candidate. However, where one partner in fact directed and
controlled the contributions of the partnership, that partner's
contributions $rou1d be cumulated with those nade to the same
candidate by the partnership.
3. Husbands and wives are treated as separate persons with
respect to the contribution Linits of the Act. Thus, if husband
and wife each controlted 50 percent of a partnership,
contributions made by the partnership and either spouse would only
be cunulated with contributions nade by the spouse who in fact
directs and controls the contributions of the partnership.
4. The number of partners j,s not relevant as to srhether
curnulation is required under the Act. The test is whether one
person in fact directs and controls the partnership's
contributions .
DI SCUSS ION
The Act, as arnended by.Proposition 73, provides that
contributions from personsr/ to candidates for elective office are
Iinited to 51,OOO pLr fiscal year,11 (Section 85301(a).) However,
under some circumstances the Act requires that contributions nade
by more than one person be cumulated and the persons treated as a
single contributor to deterrnine if they have reached the contribu-
tion linits of the Act.
Cunulation of contributions is requi.red under t$/o lines of
authority. First, in L976, the Commission set out standards for
3/ rrPersontr means an individual, proprietorship, firm, partner-
ship, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corpora-
tion, association, committee, and labor organization. (Section
8s102 (b) . )
4/ The fiscal year is July 1 through June 30. (section 85102(a).


File No. I-9O-L44
Page 3
the cumulation of contributions in two opj.nions, fn re Lunsdon
(L976) 2 FPPC Ops. 14O and In re Kahn (\976) 2 FPPC Ops. 150 (cop-
ies enclosed). In the Lumsdon Opinion, the commission found
cunulation of contributions was required rrhere contributions htere
made by a corporation and the corporation's rnajority shareholder.
In the Kahn opinion the cornmission concl,uded that cunu]ati.on of
contributions r./as required where contributions were rnade by a par-
ent company and its $rholly owned subsidiary. Both of the opinions
focused on the ability of one person to control the contributions
of another.
In addition, at the commission's June 1989 rneeting, Regula-
tion 18531.5 (copy encloseq). was adopted to further clarify when
cunulation is appropriate.5/ specifically, where the questj,on
concerns two contributors nhich are both business entities,
Regulation 18531.5 requires cunulation under the following
circumstances:
(a) If the same person or a majority of the
same persons in fact directs and controls the deci-
sions of trro or roore entities to make contributions
or expenditures to support or oppose a candidate or
candidates for elective office, those affiliated
entities shall be considered one person, one
political connittee, or one broad based political
conrnittee for purposes of the contribution linita-
tions in Government Code Sections 85301, 85302,
85303 and 85305.
(b) Business entities in a parent-subsidiary
retationship and business entities with the same
controlling (nore than so-percent) owner shall be
considered one person for purposes of the contribu-
tion limitations in GovernDent Code sections 85301,
a53o2, 85303 and 85305, unless the business enti-
ties act conpletely independently in their deci-
sions to make contributions and expenditures to
support or oppose candidates for elective office.
For purposes of this section, a parent-subsidiary
relationship exists when one business entity owns
nore than 50 percent of another business entity.
When considered together, these two lines of authority lead
to the conclusion that unless one person or a majorj.ty of the same
persons in fact directs and controls the contrj,butions of another
person to the same candidate, cumul.ation is not required. 
(Recht
Jt Regulation 18531.5 will be subnitted to the Office of
Adninistrative Law for review. Although not currently effective,
the regmlation expresses the Commission,s policy concerning
cumulation of contributions.


File No. I-90-144
Page 4
Advice Letter, No. I-89-571; Leidiqh Advice Letter, No. I-89-637;
Pines Advice Letter, I-89-7o3, copies encl-osed.)
as applied to your hypotheticals, absent sorne indication that
any of the individual partners in fact directs and control,s the
contributions of the partnership, cumulation of the partnership's
contributions and the contributions of the individual partners
would not be required. Consequently, the partnership may
contribute $1,0o0 to a candidate in a f j-scal year. In addition,
the individual partners rnay independently make contributions of
$1,0oo to the sane candidate during the same fiscal year.
Finally, husbands and wives are treated as separate persons
with respect to the contribution limits of the Act. 
(Cl,endenin
Advice Letter, No. I-89-523, copy enclosed.) Thus, husband and
wife can each Dake contributions of 51,000 to the same candidate.
If husband and wife each controlled 50 percent of a partnership,
curnutation of contributions nade by the partnership and either
individual rrould only be required rrhere one of the individuals in
fact directs and controls the contributions of the partnership.
However, please note that each contribution made by the
partnership must be analyzed separately to determine j,f any one
partner in fact directed or controlled that specific contribution.
I trust this letter has addressed your guestions. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter or a specific
decision that you would like advice on, please feel free to
contact ne at (916) 322-59OL.
Sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
counsel
Ceneral
---,_
. wallace
1, Legal, Division
KED: {W:p1h
Enc losures
